title: Cognitive Biases in PM Decisions
summary: How mental shortcuts quietly distort estimates, risk reads and stakeholder judgement, and the disciplined habits that counter them. Framed for the PMBOK 8th Edition: biases attack decisions in the Risk performance domain and in monitoring and control, and the Be an Accountable Leader principle plus structured process are the antidote.

sections.heading: What cognitive biases are
sections.body:
Cognitive biases are systematic errors in judgement that come from heuristics - the mental shortcuts the brain uses to decide quickly. They feel rational and fast, but they skew estimates, risk assessments and how we read stakeholders.

They are predictable, not random. The same mistakes recur, which is good news: once you can name a bias you can design a check against it.

This guide covers individual judgement error. Group failures such as groupthink and the halo effect are handled in the Group Decision-Making guide and the glossary, so see those for voting and facilitation.

blocks.title: Why this matters
lines: Biases are predictable, so they are defensible against.
Name the bias first - naming it lets you design a specific check.
The fix is process, not willpower: build the check into how you decide.

blocks.title: PMBOK 8th Edition lens
lines: Biases most damage the Risk domain (risk reads) and monitoring and control (status reads).
Be an Accountable Leader principle: act with integrity - report the true picture, not the comfortable one.
Be an Accountable Leader principle: invite dissent and weigh evidence over confidence.
Adopt a Holistic View principle: match the rigour of the debiasing check to the stakes of the decision.

blocks.title: Core rule
blocks.text: If a judgement feels obvious and effortless, that is exactly when to slow down and check it.

sections.heading: Bias -> effect -> counter
sections.body: The high-frequency biases that hit PM decisions, each paired with the distortion it causes and the disciplined counter-move.

rows.value:
Effect: expect things to go better than they realistically will.
Counter: use the outside view and base ranges on evidence.

Effect: underestimate own time and cost despite past overruns.
Counter: reference-class forecasting from comparable past projects.

Effect: ranges narrowed until they feel certain.
Counter: three-point / range estimates keep uncertainty visible.

Effect: over-weight the first number heard (budget, target date).
Counter: estimate independently before the anchor enters the room.

Effect: favour evidence that fits the belief you hold; read green into red signals.
Counter: ask disconfirming questions - what would prove me wrong?

Effect: continue a failing effort because of money/time already spent.
Counter: ignore sunk cost; decide only on future value.

Effect: pour in more to justify what is already gone.
Counter: set kill criteria in advance; review against them.

Effect: over-weight vivid, memorable events in the risk register.
Counter: ground risk on data and base rates, not memorability.

Effect: treat the last incident as the biggest threat.
Counter: review the full register, not just recent headlines.

Effect: low competence overrates itself and sounds confident.
Counter: weight input by evidence and track record, not volume.

Effect: high competence underrates itself; capable people stay quiet.
Counter: draw out hedged expert views; ask for the reasoning.

blocks.title: Read the table this way
lines: Every bias has a named counter - the counter is the exam-answer behaviour.
When two options both sound reasonable, pick the one that adds a disciplined check.
Distrust any option that leans on gut feel, a single number, or 'we've already invested'.

sections.heading: Estimation biases in detail
sections.body:
Optimism bias expects things to go better than they realistically will. The planning fallacy is its classic form: we underestimate the time and cost of our own work even when we know similar tasks ran over. Overconfidence then narrows our ranges so they feel certain.

Example: a team commits to a four-week migration because the happy path is four weeks, ignoring that every past migration slipped.

Anchoring compounds this - the first number heard becomes a mental peg, and later estimates drift toward it even when the peg was arbitrary. Example: a sponsor says 'we are thinking around 100k', and every quote that follows clusters near 100k regardless of true scope.

items.title: Take the outside view
lines: Ask how long this took last time, not how long it should take.
Base the estimate on comparable past projects, not the inside story.

items.title: Use reference-class forecasting
lines: Anchor on the actual outcomes of a class of similar work.
Adjust from that baseline, not from a blank page.

items.title: Estimate before the anchor lands
lines: Have estimators produce their own figures first.
Only then compare against the sponsor's proposed number.

items.title: Keep uncertainty visible
lines: Use three-point and range estimates, not single numbers.
A tight range that feels certain is a warning sign, not comfort.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Form your estimate before you let the sponsor's number into the room, and quote a range.

sections.heading: Sunk cost, confirmation and risk-read biases
sections.body:
These biases quietly corrupt the Risk domain and monitoring and control - the risk register and the status report.

Confirmation bias favours evidence that fits a belief you already hold while discounting evidence that does not; it shows up as reading a green status into red-flag signals. The sunk-cost fallacy continues a failing effort because of money, time or effort already spent, and escalation of commitment is the spiral that follows. Availability and recency distort the register so the last vivid incident is treated as the biggest threat while quieter, more probable risks are under-rated.

groups.label: Looks like
lines: Dismissing a slipping test pass rate as a blip because the plan says delivery is fine.

groups.label: Counter
lines: Ask what would prove me wrong.
Invite reviewers not invested in the current plan.
Make someone argue the project is in trouble.

groups.label: Looks like
lines: Pushing on with an unviable vendor build because 'we have already spent six figures'.

groups.label: Counter
lines: Past spend is gone whatever you choose.
Decide only on cost-to-finish vs expected benefit.
Set kill criteria before you start.

groups.label: Looks like
lines: One dramatic outage dominates planning while a more probable supplier risk is ignored.

groups.label: Counter
lines: Rate risks on probability and impact.
Use data and base rates, not memorability.
Review the whole register each cycle.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Sunk costs are gone whatever you choose; decide on the money still ahead.
Rate risks on probability and impact, not on how recent or scary they feel.

sections.heading: Self-assessment biases
sections.body:
Dunning-Kruger describes how low competence tends to overrate itself: people who know little also lack the knowledge to see how little they know, so they sound confident. Impostor syndrome is the mirror image, where high competence underrates itself and capable people doubt their own judgement.

Both distort whose voice carries weight in a room. The loudest, most confident estimate is not automatically the best informed. Example: a junior gives a firm delivery date with no doubt while the experienced engineer hedges - weigh the evidence, not the confidence.

rows.value: Low skill overrates itself; sounds certain.
High skill underrates itself; hedges or stays quiet.
Draw out the quiet expert; probe the confident claim for evidence.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Weight input by evidence and track record, not by how certain it sounds - a Be an Accountable Leader behaviour.

sections.heading: Debiasing toolkit
sections.body: Practical defences for high-stakes calls. The theme is the same across the PMBOK 8th Edition: build the check into the process so good judgement does not depend on remembering to be objective.

items.title: Premortem
lines: Imagine the project has already failed; work backwards to list why.
Surfaces risks that optimism would hide.

items.title: Structured, independent estimates
lines: Gather views separately before discussion.
Blocks anchoring and groupthink before they set in.

items.title: Devil's advocate
lines: Assign someone to argue against the favoured plan.
Legitimises dissent - a Be an Accountable Leader behaviour.

items.title: Checklists
lines: Encode the questions you always forget.
Judgement stops relying on memory.

items.title: Set kill criteria early
lines: Agree in advance what evidence ends the effort.
Neutralises sunk cost and escalation.

items.title: Slow down when it matters
lines: Reserve deliberate, evidence-based thinking for decisions that count.
Let fast intuition handle the trivial ones.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Build the check into the process so it does not depend on remembering to be objective - this is disciplined accountable leadership, and usually the correct answer.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded and restructured for the current ECO-based PMP exam and aligned to PMBOK 8th Edition principles and performance domains.
